titleTag: Missouri Landlord Tenant Law 2026 | Rights, Deposits & Eviction
metaDescription: Missouri landlord tenant law 2026: security deposit limits, eviction notice requirements, tenant rights & more. Plain-language guide for new landlords.

sections.heading: Security deposit rules
body: If you're looking for Missouri security deposit rules, focus on (1) whether there is a cap, (2) when the deposit must be returned, and (3) whether an itemized statement is required.
bullets: Deposit cap: 2 months' rent
Return deadline: 30 days after move-out/tenancy end (deductions can affect details).
Itemized statement: required if deductions are taken (check deadlines and delivery method).
Deductions: - Permissible deductions: Unpaid rent, damage beyond normal wear and tear.
Penalty risk: Varies — confirm current statute.
Interest: Varies — confirm current statute.

sections.heading: Eviction notice requirements
body: For Missouri eviction notice requirements, the notice period often depends on why the eviction is being pursued (nonpayment vs lease violation vs holdover). Always use the correct notice form and follow the court process.
bullets: Nonpayment notice: commonly 30 days (verify by tenancy type/notice form).
Nonpayment notice type: - Nonpayment of rent: Most jurisdictions do NOT require pre-litigation notice — landlord may file immediately. Some require a 5-day notice to quit.
Lease violation notice: commonly 30 days (may allow cure).
Lease violation notice type: - Lease violation: Landlord must give tenant notice and opportunity to cure. Time period depends on the nature of the violation.
Cure allowed: varies
Month-to-month termination: commonly 30 days (verify tenancy type).
Holdover/termination notice type: - Month-to-month termination: 30 days written notice required.
Court process: judicial eviction required (no self-help).
Self-help eviction: prohibited (generally requires a court order).
Timeline: - Eviction timeline: Uncontested cases approximately 2-4 weeks.
Court steps: Mo. Rev. Stat. §§ 441.510–441.880

sections.heading: Landlord entry notice
body: If you're searching for Missouri landlord entry notice rules, look for (1) required notice time, (2) allowed entry reasons, and (3) emergency exceptions.
bullets: Notice: often governed by lease terms and a reasonableness standard (state law may not set a single hour-count).
Typical notice time: varies; many leases use 24 hours as a practical baseline.

Allowed reasons: - Entry for repairs: Landlord may enter to make repairs, inspections, or to supply necessary services. - Entry to show: Landlord may enter to show the premises to prospective tenants during the last 30 days of tenancy.
Timing: - Entry times: Entry should be at reasonable times during normal business hours.
Emergencies: generally allow entry without advance notice.
Tenant refusal: varies
Remedies/penalties: Varies — confirm remedies (injunction, damages, attorney fees) under state law.

sections.heading: Rent increase rules
body: For Missouri rent increase rules, most states focus on notice requirements, while rent caps are usually local (or limited to specific covered housing).
bullets: Statewide rent control: exists (coverage and cap formulas vary).
Local rent control: may exist in certain cities/counties (check if your property is covered).
Notice of rent increase: varies — confirm current statute and any local ordinances.
Fixed-term leases: - Fixed-term leases: Rent cannot be increased during the lease term.
Month-to-month: - Rent increases: No statutory limit on amount or frequency for month-to-month tenancies.
Delivery: - Notice required: For month-to-month tenancies, at least 30 days advance written notice.
Just cause: not required
Retaliation: Retaliation protections may limit rent increases after protected tenant complaints; confirm local/state rules.

sections.heading: Tenant rights & habitability basics
body: Most Missouri landlord tenant law disputes involve habitability, repairs, and retaliation protections. Keep repair requests documented and respond promptly.
bullets: Habitability: implied warranty generally applies
Landlord repair duty: - Landlord's duty: Maintain structural components, plumbing, heating, electrical systems; provide running water and hot water; comply with housing codes.
Tenant notice: - Tenant's remedies:
Repair-and-deduct: may be allowed under conditions
Rent withholding: not allowed / very limited
Termination: 3. Termination: Tenant may terminate the rental agreement if conditions substantially impair health or safety.
Retaliation: retaliation is prohibited
Code enforcement: Varies — contact local housing/code enforcement (city/county) for inspections and official repair orders.

jurisdictionNotes: Local ordinances (city/county) can be stricter than state law.
Rules can change; verify before acting on any single requirement.
confidenceNotes: This page is being refreshed for accuracy; key limits and notice periods are verified state-by-state during the rollout.
